title: Antipsychotics and Kundalini: When Medication Is and Isn't Appropriate

body:
Antipsychotics and Kundalini: When Medication Is and Isn't Appropriate

You're probably reading this because you're scared. You're feeling things, seeing things, hearing things that don't make sense. Your mind is racing, or maybe it's completely blank. You're wondering if you're losing it, if you're actually going crazy. And someone - a doctor, a friend, maybe even your own panicked thoughts - has brought up antipsychotics. You're probably terrified of them, of what they'll do, of what they mean for your spiritual path. I get it. I’ve been there. That deep, soul-shaking fear that you're broken, and that a pill is the only answer. Or worse, that a pill will shut down whatever amazing, terrifying thing is happening to you.

Look, I'm not a doctor. I'm not here to tell you what to do medically. But I've walked this path. I've been through the absolute hell of a full-blown kundalini crisis, where my reality shattered into a million pieces. I know what it feels like when the spiritual experience veers into something that looks a hell of a lot like psychosis. And I know the struggle with medication, the fear, the relief, the confusion. So, let's talk about it, raw and real.

The Fine Line: Kundalini vs. Psychosis

Here's the thing: sometimes, what you're experiencing looks exactly like a psychotic break to someone who doesn't understand spiritual awakening. You might be hearing voices, seeing visions, having paranoid thoughts, feeling like you're God, or a prophet, or that you're being persecuted. Your sleep schedule is shot. Your emotions are all over the place. You can't function. You're convinced you have some special mission, or that the world is ending, or that you're dead. Right?

When this happens, especially if you go to an emergency room or a regular psychiatrist, they're going to see a textbook case of psychosis. They're going to diagnose you with something like bipolar disorder with psychotic features, or schizophrenia, or schizoaffective disorder. And their first, often only, tool in the toolbox for that is antipsychotics. They're not wrong from their perspective. They're trying to help you, to stabilize you, to bring you back to "normal."

But you know, deep down, that it's different. Or at least, you hope it is. You feel a spiritual component, a sense of awakening, even if it's terrifying. You might have been meditating, doing yoga, breathwork, or some other spiritual practice. And now, this. This unholy mess. You're caught between two worlds: the medical one that wants to medicate you into submission, and the spiritual one that feels like it's ripping you apart.

The books that helped me understand this distinction, that there's a place where spiritual experience and mental health crisis overlap, were things like Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link). It helps you see that you're not alone, that this isn't just you going mad, but a recognized, albeit rare, phenomenon.

When Antipsychotics Might Be Necessary

Let's be blunt: sometimes, you need them. And there's no shame in that. I've been there. If you're:

  • A danger to yourself or others: If you're having active suicidal thoughts with a plan, or if you're a threat to someone else, you need immediate intervention. Period.
  • Completely unable to function: You can't eat, sleep, communicate, or care for yourself. You're wandering around, incoherent. You're a mess.
  • Experiencing extreme, debilitating paranoia or terror: So much so that you can't even process what's happening, let alone try to integrate it.
  • Having prolonged, unmanageable psychosis: Weeks or months of intense, delusional states that aren't resolving on their own.

In these situations, antipsychotics can be a lifeline. They can bring you back to earth, even if it's just a little bit. They can quiet the noise, stop the racing thoughts, and help you get some sleep. That stability can be crucial. It can give you a chance to breathe, to ground yourself, and to begin to process what's happening. Think of it like this: if you're drowning in a storm, you need a rescue boat first, not a swimming lesson. The medication can be that rescue boat.

I remember one night, I was so out of my mind, convinced I was dying and being reborn every few seconds, that I couldn't even tell if I was breathing. My body was shaking uncontrollably, my mind was a whirlwind of cosmic horror and bliss. I was terrified. A small dose of medication, just enough to bring me down from that peak, allowed me to sleep for the first time in days. That sleep was a godsend. It didn't stop the kundalini, but it gave my system a much-needed break. Know what I mean?

The Double-Edged Sword: The Downsides and Concerns

But they're not without their problems, and this is where the fear comes in. Antipsychotics can:

  • Blunt emotions: They can make you feel flat, numb, like a zombie. This is often the biggest fear for people experiencing spiritual awakening, because you want to feel everything, not nothing.
  • Cause significant side effects: Weight gain, metabolic issues, movement disorders (akathisia, tardive dyskinesia), sedation, sexual dysfunction. These are real, and they can be awful.
  • Suppress spiritual experience: This is the big one, right? You're worried it'll shut down the kundalini, that it'll stop your awakening. And sometimes, it does, at least temporarily. It can make the spiritual sensations feel distant, muted, or just disappear.
  • Be hard to get off: Withdrawal can be brutal, especially if you've been on them for a long time or at high doses.
  • Lead to misdiagnosis: If you're diagnosed with a lifelong mental illness based on a spiritual crisis, it can be hard to shake that label, even if your experience resolves.

So yeah, it's a tough call. You're damned if you do, damned if you don't sometimes. You're weighing the immediate need for stability against the potential long-term effects and the fear of losing your spiritual connection.

When Antipsychotics Might NOT Be Appropriate (or need careful consideration)

This is where things get tricky, and where you need to advocate for yourself, hard. If you're experiencing a kundalini awakening, and you're not in immediate danger, but you're being pushed towards heavy medication, you need to pump the brakes and ask questions. Here's why:

  • You're still able to function, even if it's difficult: You're scared, you're having intense experiences, but you can still eat, sleep (even if it's not well), hold a conversation, and care for your basic needs.
  • Your experiences, while intense, aren't completely delusional or disorganized: You might be seeing auras or hearing subtle sounds, but you're not fully convinced you're Jesus reincarnated and need to save the world tomorrow. You still have a foot in reality.
  • You're looking for support, not suppression: You want to understand what's happening, to integrate it, not just shut it down.
  • The doctor isn't listening to your spiritual context: If they're dismissing your talk of kundalini, awakening, or spiritual experiences as "delusions" without any attempt to understand, that's a red flag.

In these cases, a lower dose, a different type of medication (maybe an anxiolytic or a mood stabilizer if anxiety or mood swings are the primary issue), or even no medication, might be a better path. The goal should be to provide enough support to manage the crisis without completely extinguishing the underlying spiritual process. It's about finding a balance. It's about harm reduction, not necessarily eradication of the experience.

Some practitioners, particularly those familiar with spiritual emergencies, might suggest very low doses, or short-term use, to help you through the worst of it. They might also suggest things like grounding practices, diet changes, therapy with someone who understands spiritual awakening, and connecting with a community that validates your experience. This is what you want. This is the ideal.

Books like When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) and The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) talk about this, about navigating the process without necessarily shutting it down. And The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) offers case studies that show the wide range of experiences, some of which were mistaken for psychosis.

My Own Experience and What I Learned

I was offered antipsychotics. Heavily. I resisted for a long time, out of fear. And my crisis got worse. I ended up in a mental hospital, utterly broken. They gave me a tiny dose of an antipsychotic, along with other meds for anxiety and sleep. It wasn't a magic bullet, but it was enough to take the edge off, to quiet the terror just enough so I could start to breathe. It didn't stop the kundalini, not really. It just made it manageable. It felt like someone had turned the volume down from 11 to about 7. Still loud, still intense, but I could hear myself think.

Later, I slowly, carefully, with the help of a very understanding doctor, tapered off everything. It was a long, hard road. But I did it. And the kundalini was still there, still working, still doing its thing. The medication hadn't killed it. It had just given me a chance to survive the storm.

So, what's my point? My point is, don't be afraid to consider them if you're truly in crisis. Don't let the spiritual community's sometimes rigid anti-medication stance make you suffer unnecessarily. But also, don't let the medical community slap a label on you and medicate you into oblivion if you don't need it. It's a tightrope walk, and you're the one walking it.

Practical Steps You Can Take

  1. Educate yourself: Read books like the ones I mentioned. Understand what kundalini crisis is. This knowledge is your shield.
  2. Seek out a doctor who is open-minded: This is hard, I know. But try to find a psychiatrist or therapist who is at least willing to listen to your spiritual perspective, even if they don't fully understand it. Someone who won't immediately dismiss it as delusion.
  3. Consider a lower dose: If medication is recommended, ask about the lowest effective dose. Question high doses, especially if you're not in immediate danger.
  4. Short-term vs. Long-term: Ask if this is meant to be a short-term intervention or a long-term solution. For a spiritual crisis, short-term stabilization is often the goal.
  5. Focus on grounding: Whether you take medication or not, grounding practices are vital. Connect with nature, eat nourishing food, get some gentle exercise, spend time with supportive, calm people.
  6. Build a support system: Connect with others who have been through similar experiences. You are not alone, even if it feels like it at 3 AM.
  7. Listen to your intuition: Your body and spirit will tell you what's right, even if it's a whisper through the chaos.

Look, you're going through something immense. Something that most people can't even fathom. It's okay to need help. It's okay to be scared. It's okay to use whatever tools are available to you to get through this. The goal isn't to be "pure" or "unmedicated" if it means suffering horribly. The goal is to survive, to integrate, and to eventually thrive on the other side of this. Does that land?

So yeah, be smart. Be brave. And above all, be kind to yourself. You're doing the best you can in an impossible situation.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
